equipment.description: Portable Prime Water Pump Diesel Engine

background.text:
BACKGROUND
The Pittsburg Owner  LPV, LLC  has applied  for an Authority to Construct/Permit to Operate for 
the following equipment:  
 
S-75 Portable Prime Water Pump Diesel Engine  
Tier 4 Final  with integrated SCR ; Make: John Deere ; Model: 6068CI550 ;  
Model Year: 2024 
277 brake horsepower ( bhp), 1.61 Million British Thermal Units per hour 
(MMBtu/hr ) 
CARB Executive Order: U -R-004-0676 
 
S-75 will operate at various locations within the Pittsburg Owner site at 696 W 10th Street in 
Pittsburg. S -75 will drive a water pump that will be used to manage stormwater at the various 
locations within the facility boundary.  S-75 meet s the Environmental Protection Agency and 
California Air Resources Board (EPA/CARB) Tier 4 Final  Off-road standard s for compression 
ignited engines . The engine will burn commercially available California low sulfur diesel fuel. The 
sulfur content of the diesel fuel will not exceed 0.0015% by weight.   
 
Portable engines are non -road engines as defined by 40 CFR 10393.801 and 1068.30.  Section 
209(e)(1) of the Federal Clean Air Act does not allow states “or political subdivisions” to impose 
emission control on non -road engines. The exception is standards a nd other requirements imposed 
by the State of California necessary to achieve attainment of air pollution standards. The regulatory 
analysis in this application will take this into account.  
 
This evaluation report will discuss compliance with all applicable rules and regulations for the 
proposed project.

emission_calculations.text:
EMISSIONS
NS  
The criteria pollutants expected from the combustion of diesel fuel are Oxides of Nitrogen (NO x), 
Precursor Organic Compounds (POC), carbon monoxide (CO), particulate matter with an 
aerodynamic diameter of 10 microns (PM 10), particulate matter with an aerodynamic diameter of 
2.5 microns (PM 2.5), and sulfur  dioxide  (SO 2). NO x, POC, CO and PM 10 emission  factors  are 
sourced from the CARB Executive Order U -R-004-0676 ; PM 2.5 emissions are assumed to be equal 
to PM 10 emissions;  and the SO 2 potential to emit (PTE)  was estimated based on the sulfur content 
of CARB regulated ultra -low sulfur diesel at 1 5 part per million by weight ( ppmw) sulfur as 
follows:  
 
PTE SO2 = 0.000015 pound -S/pound -diesel × 7.1 pound -diesel/gal lon × 2-pound -SO 2/pound -S × 
Fuel use (gal/hr)   
 
 
 2 Maximum daily emissions are estimated  assuming 24 hours of operation per day (hr/day) and 
annual emissions are estimated assuming  1,920  hours  of operation  per year  (hr/year) . Emission 
factors are presented in grams per brake horsepower -hour (g/bhp -hr) and emission rates are 
expressed as pounds per day (lb/day), pounds per year (lb/year), or tons per year (ton/year).

toxic_risk_screening_analysis.narrative:
Diesel  Exhaust  Particulate Matter (D EPM) is the primary Toxic Air Contaminant (TAC) of
concern from diesel combustion sources. D EPM emissions are equivalent to the PM 10/2.5 emissions 
estimated from S-75. Ammonia slip emissions are expected because S -75 is equipped with an 
integral Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR) system to abate NO x emissions. Ammonia slipHealth Risk Assessment is required
4 S-75 is a portable engine that will operate at various locations within the boundary of Facility 
24926. The engine may operate  within 1,000 feet of the outer boundary of a K -12 school site  (St. 
Peter Martyr School)  and the engine  will be operated  within an OBC . As seen in the Regulation 
2-5 discussion below, a H ealth Risk Assessment  was required for this application.  Therefore, a 30-
day OBC and Waters Bill public notic e will be required prior to issuance of the A uthority to 
Construct  for this application . 
 
Regulation 2, Rule 2 – New Source Review  
2-2-301 – Best Available Control Technology (BACT)  
S-75 is a portable engine. Therefore, emissions from this source are not subject to BACT standards 
per Section 209(e) of the Federal Clean Air Act and Appendix A to Subpart A of 40 CFR Part 
1074.  
 
2-2-302 – Offset Requirements, Precursor Organic Compounds and Oxides of Nitrogen  
Offsets are required for new and modified sources when the potential to emit NO x or POC at a 
facility exceeds 10 tons per year  per pollutant . As seen in Table 4, the facility ’s PTE does not  
exceed 10 tons per year for NO x or POC.  
 
Table 4 – NO x and POC Offsets  
Pollutant  Current PTE 
(ton/year)  Application 
720694  
PTE1 
(ton/year)  PTE Post -
Application 
(ton/year)  Offset 
Threshold 
(ton/year)  Offsets 
Required?  
NO x 0.000  0.176 0.176  10 No 
POC  0.055  0.082 0.137  No 
1 The proposed permit conditions will limit S -75’s hours of operation to 1,920 hours/year. Therefore, S -75’s PTE in Table 4 
was estimated per 2-1-217.  
 
2-2-303 – Offset requirements, PM 2.5, PM 10, and Sulfur Dioxide  
Per Section 2 -2-303, offsets are required for a new or modified source at a facility that will have a 
potential to emit more than 100 tons or more per year of PM 2.5, PM 10, or sulfur dioxide  for each of 
the above pollutants after the new or modified source is constructed. As seen in Table 5, the facility 
PTE does not equal or exceed 100 tons per year for any of the above three pollutants.  
 
Table 5 – PM 2.5, PM 10, and SO 2 Offsets

100 No

SO 2 0.000 0.003 0.003  No 
1 The proposed permit conditions will limit S -75’s hours of operation to 1,920 hours/year. Therefore, S -75’s PTE in Table 5 
was estimated per 2 -1-217.  
 
 
 5 2-2-304, 2 -2-305, and 2 -2-306 – Prevention of Significant Deterioration ( PSD) BACT and 
Impact Analysis Requirements  
Sections 2 -2-304, 2 -2-305, and 2 -2-306 outline BACT and Impact Analysis requirements for PSD 
projects. Section 2 -2-224 defines a PSD Project as one that meets all of the following criteria: (i) 
The sources are located at a Major PSD facility; (ii) The project results in a significant emissions 
increase per Section 2 -2-227.1; (iii) The project results in a s ignificant net emissions increase per 
Section 2 -2-227.1.  
 
The total emissions  increase from S -75, summarized  in Table 1, are below the significant emission 
rate thresholds for each pollutant in Section 2 -2-227.2. Therefore, PSD does not apply.Regulation 2, Rule 5 – New Source Review of Toxic Air Contaminants
2-5-301 and 2-5-302 – Best Available Control Technology for Toxics (TBACT) and Project Risk 
Requirements  
As seen in Table 2  assuming S -75 operates within  the Pittsburg Owner site at 696 W 10th Street in 
Pittsburg , CA , the annual D EPM emissions exceed the chronic trigger level in Table 2 -5-1. 
Therefore , a Health Risk Assessment (HRA)  was conducted to determine compliance with TBACT 
and the project risk standards of Sections 2 -5-301 and 2 -5-302. 
 
HRA Results  
The HRA Report dated April 22, 2025  concluded…The HRA “…estimates the incremental health risk resulting from toxic air contaminant (TAC)
emissions from a new portable prime water pump diesel engine (S -75) at this facility. Results from  
this HRA  indicate that the project cancer risk is estimated at 5.9 in a million, the project chronic 
hazard index (HI) is estimated at 0.0016, and the project acute HI is estimate at 0.00061.  Since the 
estimated project cancer risk does not exceed 6.0 in a million, this proposed project complies with 
the District’s Regul ation 2 -5-302 project risk requirements, for projects located in an 
Overburdened  Community, as defined in Regulation 2 -1-243.”7 Airborne Toxic Control Measure for Diesel Particulate Matter from Portable Engines
s 
Rated at 50 Horsepower and Greater (ATCM)  
S-75 is expected to comply with the fuel requirements per §93116.3(a) by using only CARB 
certified diesel fuel to fuel  the engine.  
 
S-75 is subject to and meet s the emission standards in § 93116.3(b). § 93116.3(b)(1) requires all 
portable diesel fueled engines to be certified to meet a federal or California standard for newly 
manufactured engines pursuant to 40 CFR Part 1039 or the equivalent categories in Title 13 of the 
California Code o f Regulations. The applicable standards from 40 CFR Part 1039 for S -75 are 
discussed below : 
 
For engines rated greater than or equal to 175 and less than or equal to 750 bhp with model year 
2015 or newer, the applicable emission standards from Table 1 of 40 CFR Part 1039.101 are:  
 
• NO x:  0.30 g/bhp -hr 
• CO:  2.61 g/bhp -hr 
• PM:  0.015 g/bhp -hr 
• NMHC  0.14 g/bhp -hr 
 
Based on NO x, CO , PM 10, and POC (~NMHC)  emission factors sourced from CARB Executive 
Order U -R-004-0676 and cited in Table 1 of this report , S-75 (277 bhp)  compl ies with the portable 
ATCM.  
 
New Source Performance Standards (NSPS)  
40 CFR Part 60, Subpart IIII, Standards of Performance for Stationary Compression Ignition 
Internal Combustion Engines applies to stationary reciprocating internal combustion engines. S -
75 is not considered a stationary reciprocating combustion engine because it is portable and can 
move around the site to pump water  at various locations. Therefore, S -75 is not subject to any parts 
of 40 CFR Part 60, Subpart IIII.  
 
National Emissions Standards for Hazardous Air Pollutants (NESHAP)  
40 CFR Part 63, Subpart ZZZZ, National Emission Standards for Hazardous Air Pollutants for 
Reciprocating Internal Combustion Engines (RICE) applies to stationary reciprocating internal 
combustion engines. S -75 is not considered a stationary reciprocating combustion engine because 
it is portable and can move around the site to pump water  at various locations. Therefore, S -75 is 
not subject to any parts of 40 CFR Part 63, Subpart ZZZZ.

findings:
STATEMENT OF COMPLIANCE
CE  
The owner/operator is expected to comply with all applicable requirements. Key requirements are 
listed below:  
 
Regulation 2, Rule 1 – General Requirements  
2-1-310 – Applicability of CEQA  
Although CO emissions from S -75 exceed 10.0 pounds per day, B est Available Control 
Technology  is not triggered because S -75 is a portable engine as discussed in the Regulation 2, 
Rule 2 section below. This p ermit application is  not subject to the  California Environmental 
Quality Act (CEQA)  because the Air District’s evaluation is a ministerial action (Public Resources 
Code Section 21080(b)(1) and CEQA Guidelines Section 15268(a)) conducted using fixed 
standards and objective measurements in the Air District’s Rules and Regulations .

public_notification.text:
2-1-412 – Public Notification, Schools
Public notification is required for the following:  
(a) New or modified sources that result in an increase in hazardous air emissions located within 
1,000 feet of the outer boundary of a K -12 school site.  
(b) New or modified sources located within an Overburdened Community  (OBC)  for which a

conditions.text:
Permit Conditions
ns  
 
Permit Condition # 100705 for S -75 
 
1. To retain the portable source and nonroad engine  designations, the owner/operator of S -75 
shall not operate the engine in one location as defined in § 93116.2 for more than 12 
consecutive months, following the date of initial operation. Any backup engine, which 
replaces S -75 at the same location and that is intended to perform the same function as the 
engine it replaced will count towards this limitation. The owner/operator of S -75 shall not 
 
 8 move the source and then return it back to the same location in an attempt to circumvent the 
portable equipment residence time requirements. The owner/operator of S -75 shall operate 
the engine  within the boundaries of Facility # 24926 . If S -75 is no longer a portable source as 
defined in § 93116.2, the owner/operator shall cease operating the  engine  immediately. The 
owner/operator may submit an application to BAAQMD to operate the engine as  a stationary 
source. In this case, the engine shall be subject to BA AQMD Regulation 2, Rules 1, 2, and 5 
as if it was  a “New Source” per BAAQMD Regulation 2, Rule 1 , Section 232 . 
[Basis:  Regulation 2, Rule 5, 17 CCR 93116 ] 
 
2. The owner/operator of S-75 shall fire the engine  exclusively on CARB diesel fuel.  
[Basis: Cumulative Increase, Regulation 2 -5, 17 CCR 9311 6.3(a) ] 
 
3. The owner/operator of S -75 shall not operate the engine for more than 1,920 hours in any 
consecutive 12 -month period.  
[Basis: Cumulative Increase; Regulation 2 -5-302] 
 
4. The owner/operator of S-75 shall equip the engine with either:  
a. A non -resettable totalizing meter that measures hours of operation for the engine; or  
b. A non -resettable fuel usage meter, the maximum hourly fuel rate shall be used to 
convert fuel usage to hours of operation.  
[Basis: Cumulative Increase ]  
 
5. To determine compliance with the above conditions, the owner /operator of S -75 shall 
maintain the following records and provide all of the data necessary to evaluate compliance 
with the above conditions, including the following information:  
a. Monthly consumption of fuel (in gallons) .  
b. Hours of operation and amount of diesel fuel in part a) shall be totaled on a rolling 
consecutive 12 -month basis .  
c. Location  at  which the engine operates and the duration of operation at each 
location .  
d. The owner/operator of S -75 shall maintain all records in an Air District -approved 
log. The owner/operator shall retain the records with the equipment for two years 
from the date of entry and make them available for inspection by Air District staff 
upon req uest. These recordkeeping requirements shall not replace the 
recordkeeping requirements in any applicable Air District Regulations.  
[Basis: Cumulative Increase, Regulation 2 -5] 
 
6. Within 30 days after the end of each calendar year, the owner/operator of S -75 shall provide 
the Director of the Air District’s Compliance and Enforcement Division a year -end summary 
with the following information :  
a. The location(s) and dates at which the engine was operated.  
b. The total amount of diesel fuel consumed by the engine in the previous 12 months 
(in gallons) . 
c. The total hours of operation.  
[Basis: Regulation 2 -1-403] 
 
 
 9  
 
 
End of Conditions

recommendation.text:
Recommendation
on  
The Air District has evaluated the permit application for the proposed project and has made a 
preliminary determination that the project is expected to comply with all applicable Air District, 
state, and federal air quality -related regulations, including t he health risks resulting from toxic air 
contaminant emissions. The preliminary recommendation is to issue a permit for this project. After 
considering all comments received, the Air District will make a final determination.  
 
I recommend that the Air District initiate the public comment period and consider any comments 
received prior to taking any final action on issuance of an Authority to Construct for the following 
source:  
 
 
S-75 Portable Prime Water Pump Diesel Engine  
Tier 4 Final with integrated SCR; Make: John Deere ; Model: 6068CI550 ;  
Model Year: 2024 
277 bhp,  1.61 MMBtu/hr  
CARB Executive Order: U -R-004-0676  
 
 
 
Prepared By:   Kevin Perez , Air Quality Engineer  I 
Date:   July 2, 202 5

blocks.heading: BACKGROUND
blocks.text:
BACKGROUND
The Pittsburg Owner  LPV, LLC  has applied  for an Authority to Construct/Permit to Operate for 
the following equipment:  
 
S-75 Portable Prime Water Pump Diesel Engine  
Tier 4 Final  with integrated SCR ; Make: John Deere ; Model: 6068CI550 ;  
Model Year: 2024 
277 brake horsepower ( bhp), 1.61 Million British Thermal Units per hour 
(MMBtu/hr ) 
CARB Executive Order: U -R-004-0676 
 
S-75 will operate at various locations within the Pittsburg Owner site at 696 W 10th Street in 
Pittsburg. S -75 will drive a water pump that will be used to manage stormwater at the various 
locations within the facility boundary.  S-75 meet s the Environmental Protection Agency and 
California Air Resources Board (EPA/CARB) Tier 4 Final  Off-road standard s for compression 
ignited engines . The engine will burn commercially available California low sulfur diesel fuel. The 
sulfur content of the diesel fuel will not exceed 0.0015% by weight.   
 
Portable engines are non -road engines as defined by 40 CFR 10393.801 and 1068.30.  Section 
209(e)(1) of the Federal Clean Air Act does not allow states “or political subdivisions” to impose 
emission control on non -road engines. The exception is standards a nd other requirements imposed 
by the State of California necessary to achieve attainment of air pollution standards. The regulatory 
analysis in this application will take this into account.  
 
This evaluation report will discuss compliance with all applicable rules and regulations for the 
proposed project.

blocks.heading: EMISSIONS
blocks.text:
EMISSIONS
NS  
The criteria pollutants expected from the combustion of diesel fuel are Oxides of Nitrogen (NO x), 
Precursor Organic Compounds (POC), carbon monoxide (CO), particulate matter with an 
aerodynamic diameter of 10 microns (PM 10), particulate matter with an aerodynamic diameter of 
2.5 microns (PM 2.5), and sulfur  dioxide  (SO 2). NO x, POC, CO and PM 10 emission  factors  are 
sourced from the CARB Executive Order U -R-004-0676 ; PM 2.5 emissions are assumed to be equal 
to PM 10 emissions;  and the SO 2 potential to emit (PTE)  was estimated based on the sulfur content 
of CARB regulated ultra -low sulfur diesel at 1 5 part per million by weight ( ppmw) sulfur as 
follows:  
 
PTE SO2 = 0.000015 pound -S/pound -diesel × 7.1 pound -diesel/gal lon × 2-pound -SO 2/pound -S × 
Fuel use (gal/hr)

annual emissions are estimated assuming  1,920  hours  of operation  per year  (hr/year) . Emission 
factors are presented in grams per brake horsepower -hour (g/bhp -hr) and emission rates are 
expressed as pounds per day (lb/day), pounds per year (lb/year), or tons per year (ton/year).

blocks.heading: Diesel  Exhaust  Particulate Matter (D EPM) is the primary Toxic Air Contaminant (TAC) of
blocks.text:
Diesel  Exhaust  Particulate Matter (D EPM) is the primary Toxic Air Contaminant (TAC) of
concern from diesel combustion sources. D EPM emissions are equivalent to the PM 10/2.5 emissions 
estimated from S-75. Ammonia slip emissions are expected because S -75 is equipped with an 
integral Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR) system to abate NO x emissions. Ammonia slip

blocks.heading: emissions are calculated as follows:
blocks.text:

• Ammonia slip is assumed to have a concentration of 10 part per million by volume ( ppmv ) 
at 15% oxygen ( O2) in the exhaust  
• The moisture content in the exhaust is assumed to be 12.5%  
• The exhaust flow rate  of 194.5 actual cubic feet per minute (acfm)  and temperature are 
sourced from the manufacturer’s specifications for S -75. Flow rate is corrected to dry 
standard cubic feet per minute (dscfm)

blocks.heading: STATEMENT OF COMPLIANCE
blocks.text:
STATEMENT OF COMPLIANCE
CE  
The owner/operator is expected to comply with all applicable requirements. Key requirements are 
listed below:  
 
Regulation 2, Rule 1 – General Requirements  
2-1-310 – Applicability of CEQA  
Although CO emissions from S -75 exceed 10.0 pounds per day, B est Available Control 
Technology  is not triggered because S -75 is a portable engine as discussed in the Regulation 2, 
Rule 2 section below. This p ermit application is  not subject to the  California Environmental 
Quality Act (CEQA)  because the Air District’s evaluation is a ministerial action (Public Resources 
Code Section 21080(b)(1) and CEQA Guidelines Section 15268(a)) conducted using fixed 
standards and objective measurements in the Air District’s Rules and Regulations .

blocks.heading: 2-1-412 – Public Notification, Schools
blocks.text:
2-1-412 – Public Notification, Schools
Public notification is required for the following:  
(a) New or modified sources that result in an increase in hazardous air emissions located within 
1,000 feet of the outer boundary of a K -12 school site.  
(b) New or modified sources located within an Overburdened Community  (OBC)  for which a

blocks.heading: Health Risk Assessment is required
blocks.text:
Health Risk Assessment is required
4 S-75 is a portable engine that will operate at various locations within the boundary of Facility 
24926. The engine may operate  within 1,000 feet of the outer boundary of a K -12 school site  (St. 
Peter Martyr School)  and the engine  will be operated  within an OBC . As seen in the Regulation 
2-5 discussion below, a H ealth Risk Assessment  was required for this application.  Therefore, a 30-
day OBC and Waters Bill public notic e will be required prior to issuance of the A uthority to 
Construct  for this application . 
 
Regulation 2, Rule 2 – New Source Review  
2-2-301 – Best Available Control Technology (BACT)  
S-75 is a portable engine. Therefore, emissions from this source are not subject to BACT standards 
per Section 209(e) of the Federal Clean Air Act and Appendix A to Subpart A of 40 CFR Part 
1074.  
 
2-2-302 – Offset Requirements, Precursor Organic Compounds and Oxides of Nitrogen  
Offsets are required for new and modified sources when the potential to emit NO x or POC at a 
facility exceeds 10 tons per year  per pollutant . As seen in Table 4, the facility ’s PTE does not  
exceed 10 tons per year for NO x or POC.  
 
Table 4 – NO x and POC Offsets

(ton/year)  Application

NO x 0.000  0.176 0.176  10 No 
POC  0.055  0.082 0.137  No 
1 The proposed permit conditions will limit S -75’s hours of operation to 1,920 hours/year. Therefore, S -75’s PTE in Table 4 
was estimated per 2-1-217.  
 
2-2-303 – Offset requirements, PM 2.5, PM 10, and Sulfur Dioxide  
Per Section 2 -2-303, offsets are required for a new or modified source at a facility that will have a 
potential to emit more than 100 tons or more per year of PM 2.5, PM 10, or sulfur dioxide  for each of 
the above pollutants after the new or modified source is constructed. As seen in Table 5, the facility 
PTE does not equal or exceed 100 tons per year for any of the above three pollutants.

1 The proposed permit conditions will limit S -75’s hours of operation to 1,920 hours/year. Therefore, S -75’s PTE in Table 5 
was estimated per 2 -1-217.  
 
 
 5 2-2-304, 2 -2-305, and 2 -2-306 – Prevention of Significant Deterioration ( PSD) BACT and 
Impact Analysis Requirements  
Sections 2 -2-304, 2 -2-305, and 2 -2-306 outline BACT and Impact Analysis requirements for PSD 
projects. Section 2 -2-224 defines a PSD Project as one that meets all of the following criteria: (i) 
The sources are located at a Major PSD facility; (ii) The project results in a significant emissions 
increase per Section 2 -2-227.1; (iii) The project results in a s ignificant net emissions increase per 
Section 2 -2-227.1.  
 
The total emissions  increase from S -75, summarized  in Table 1, are below the significant emission 
rate thresholds for each pollutant in Section 2 -2-227.2. Therefore, PSD does not apply.

blocks.heading: Regulation 2, Rule 5 – New Source Review of Toxic Air Contaminants
blocks.text:
Regulation 2, Rule 5 – New Source Review of Toxic Air Contaminants
2-5-301 and 2-5-302 – Best Available Control Technology for Toxics (TBACT) and Project Risk 
Requirements  
As seen in Table 2  assuming S -75 operates within  the Pittsburg Owner site at 696 W 10th Street in 
Pittsburg , CA , the annual D EPM emissions exceed the chronic trigger level in Table 2 -5-1. 
Therefore , a Health Risk Assessment (HRA)  was conducted to determine compliance with TBACT 
and the project risk standards of Sections 2 -5-301 and 2 -5-302. 
 
HRA Results  
The HRA Report dated April 22, 2025  concluded…

blocks.heading: The HRA “…estimates the incremental health risk resulting from toxic air contaminant (TAC)
blocks.text:
The HRA “…estimates the incremental health risk resulting from toxic air contaminant (TAC)
emissions from a new portable prime water pump diesel engine (S -75) at this facility. Results from  
this HRA  indicate that the project cancer risk is estimated at 5.9 in a million, the project chronic 
hazard index (HI) is estimated at 0.0016, and the project acute HI is estimate at 0.00061.  Since the 
estimated project cancer risk does not exceed 6.0 in a million, this proposed project complies with 
the District’s Regul ation 2 -5-302 project risk requirements, for projects located in an 
Overburdened  Community, as defined in Regulation 2 -1-243.”

blocks.heading: TBACT
blocks.text:
TBACT
CT  
Although S -75 results in a source risk that exceeds the Regulation 2 -5-301 TBACT threshold of 
1.0 in a million cancer risk, TBACT is not required for S -75 because it  is a portable non -road 
engine . Per Section 209(e) of the Federal Clean Air Act, “political subdivisions” shall not impose 
emission controls on non -road engines. The exception is for standards and other requirements 
imposed by the State of California necessary to achieve attainment of air pollution standards.  
 
Regulation 6, Rule 1 – Particulate Matter - General Requirements  
6-1-303 – Ringelmann No. 2 Limitation and 6 -1-305 – Visible Particles  
S-75 is subject to Regulation 6, Rule 1. Opacity and visible emissions from S-75 are limited by 
Regulation 6 -1-303.1 (internal combustion engines of less than 25 liters displacement). S-75 has a 
displacement of 6.8 liters.  
 
Section 6 -1-303.1 prohibits S-75 from  emitting for more than 3 minutes per hour visible emissions 
as dark or darker than Ringelmann No. 2 or equivalent opacity.  
 
 
 6 Regulation 6 -1-305 prohibits emission of particles from any operation in sufficient number to 
cause annoyance to any other person where the particles are large enough to be visible as individual 
particles at the emission point, or of such size and nature as to be visible  individually as 
incandescent particles.  S -75 is not expected to produce visible emissions or fallout in violation of 
this regulation and will be assumed to be in compliance with Regulation 6 -1-305. 
 
S-75 will comply with 0.15  grains per dry standard cubic feet  (gr/dscf ) in Section 6 -1-310.1, as 
shown below:  
 
PM 10 emission rate = 0. 0092 lb/hr (Refer to Table 2);  
 
Exhaust flow rate  = 136.15  dscfm  (194.5  acfm  @ 200 °F and 12.5% moisture content );  
Outlet grain loading rate = 0. 008 gr/dscf ( 0.0092 x 7000 ÷ 136.15  x 60) 
 
Sections 6 -1-310.2 and 6 -1-311.2 don’t apply  to S-75, since the PTE of engine to emit total 
suspended particulate ( TSP) is significantly lower than 1,000 kilograms per hour ( kg/hr ). Also, the 
above sections apply to processes that handle or process materials (not ICE).  
 
Compliance with the above standards in Regulation 6, Rule 1 will be confirmed by Air District 
Compliance and Enforcement staff during routine inspections of the facility.  
 
Regulation 9, Rule 1 – Inorganic Gaseous Pollutants - Sulfur Dioxide  
9-1-301 – Limitation on Ground Level Concentration  
S-75 is subject to and expected to comply with the applicable SO 2 limitations in Regulation 9, Rule 
1 (“Inorganic Gaseous Pollutants - Sulfur Dioxide”). Because SO 2 emissions from S-75 are 
negligible ( 5 lb/year), “The Pittsburg Owner LPV, LLC ” will not be required to conduct ground 
level monitoring of SO 2. 
 
9-1-304 – Fuel Burning (Liquid and Solid Fuels)  
Section 9 -1-304 states “A person shall not burn any liquid fuel having a sulfur content in excess 
of 0.5% by weight,” Section 209 (e) of the Federal Clean Air Act does not allow states or “political 
subdivisions” to impose emission control on non -road engi nes except when standards are 
necessary to achieve attainment of an air pollution standard, but does allow for regulating the hours 
of operation, daily emissions, and sulfur limits in the fuel. The California Air Resources Board 
(CARB) requires all diesel engines to burn Ultra Low Sulfur Diesel with a sulfur content of 15 
ppm by weight or less (≤ 0.0015% by wt.), which is less than 0.5% by wt. requirement in Section 
9-1-304. The requirement to ensure S -75 exclusively burn CARB diesel (~ULSD) is memorialized 
in Part 2 of the proposed permit condition # 100705. Therefore, S -75 will comply with 9 -1-304. 
 
Regulation 9, Rule 8 – Nitrogen Oxides and Carbon Monoxides from Stationary Internal 
Combustion Engines  
Since S -75 is portable, the  engine  is subject to Section 209(e) of the Federal Clean Air Act and is 
not subject to Regulation 9 -8 which applies to stationary ICE.

blocks.heading: 7 Airborne Toxic Control Measure for Diesel Particulate Matter from Portable Engines
blocks.text:
7 Airborne Toxic Control Measure for Diesel Particulate Matter from Portable Engines
s 
Rated at 50 Horsepower and Greater (ATCM)  
S-75 is expected to comply with the fuel requirements per §93116.3(a) by using only CARB 
certified diesel fuel to fuel  the engine.  
 
S-75 is subject to and meet s the emission standards in § 93116.3(b). § 93116.3(b)(1) requires all 
portable diesel fueled engines to be certified to meet a federal or California standard for newly 
manufactured engines pursuant to 40 CFR Part 1039 or the equivalent categories in Title 13 of the 
California Code o f Regulations. The applicable standards from 40 CFR Part 1039 for S -75 are 
discussed below : 
 
For engines rated greater than or equal to 175 and less than or equal to 750 bhp with model year 
2015 or newer, the applicable emission standards from Table 1 of 40 CFR Part 1039.101 are:  
 
• NO x:  0.30 g/bhp -hr 
• CO:  2.61 g/bhp -hr 
• PM:  0.015 g/bhp -hr 
• NMHC  0.14 g/bhp -hr 
 
Based on NO x, CO , PM 10, and POC (~NMHC)  emission factors sourced from CARB Executive 
Order U -R-004-0676 and cited in Table 1 of this report , S-75 (277 bhp)  compl ies with the portable 
ATCM.  
 
New Source Performance Standards (NSPS)  
40 CFR Part 60, Subpart IIII, Standards of Performance for Stationary Compression Ignition 
Internal Combustion Engines applies to stationary reciprocating internal combustion engines. S -
75 is not considered a stationary reciprocating combustion engine because it is portable and can 
move around the site to pump water  at various locations. Therefore, S -75 is not subject to any parts 
of 40 CFR Part 60, Subpart IIII.  
 
National Emissions Standards for Hazardous Air Pollutants (NESHAP)  
40 CFR Part 63, Subpart ZZZZ, National Emission Standards for Hazardous Air Pollutants for 
Reciprocating Internal Combustion Engines (RICE) applies to stationary reciprocating internal 
combustion engines. S -75 is not considered a stationary reciprocating combustion engine because 
it is portable and can move around the site to pump water  at various locations. Therefore, S -75 is 
not subject to any parts of 40 CFR Part 63, Subpart ZZZZ.

blocks.heading: Permit Conditions
blocks.text:
Permit Conditions
ns  
 
Permit Condition # 100705 for S -75 
 
1. To retain the portable source and nonroad engine  designations, the owner/operator of S -75 
shall not operate the engine in one location as defined in § 93116.2 for more than 12 
consecutive months, following the date of initial operation. Any backup engine, which 
replaces S -75 at the same location and that is intended to perform the same function as the 
engine it replaced will count towards this limitation. The owner/operator of S -75 shall not 
 
 8 move the source and then return it back to the same location in an attempt to circumvent the 
portable equipment residence time requirements. The owner/operator of S -75 shall operate 
the engine  within the boundaries of Facility # 24926 . If S -75 is no longer a portable source as 
defined in § 93116.2, the owner/operator shall cease operating the  engine  immediately. The 
owner/operator may submit an application to BAAQMD to operate the engine as  a stationary 
source. In this case, the engine shall be subject to BA AQMD Regulation 2, Rules 1, 2, and 5 
as if it was  a “New Source” per BAAQMD Regulation 2, Rule 1 , Section 232 . 
[Basis:  Regulation 2, Rule 5, 17 CCR 93116 ] 
 
2. The owner/operator of S-75 shall fire the engine  exclusively on CARB diesel fuel.  
[Basis: Cumulative Increase, Regulation 2 -5, 17 CCR 9311 6.3(a) ] 
 
3. The owner/operator of S -75 shall not operate the engine for more than 1,920 hours in any 
consecutive 12 -month period.  
[Basis: Cumulative Increase; Regulation 2 -5-302] 
 
4. The owner/operator of S-75 shall equip the engine with either:  
a. A non -resettable totalizing meter that measures hours of operation for the engine; or  
b. A non -resettable fuel usage meter, the maximum hourly fuel rate shall be used to 
convert fuel usage to hours of operation.  
[Basis: Cumulative Increase ]  
 
5. To determine compliance with the above conditions, the owner /operator of S -75 shall 
maintain the following records and provide all of the data necessary to evaluate compliance 
with the above conditions, including the following information:  
a. Monthly consumption of fuel (in gallons) .  
b. Hours of operation and amount of diesel fuel in part a) shall be totaled on a rolling 
consecutive 12 -month basis .  
c. Location  at  which the engine operates and the duration of operation at each 
location .  
d. The owner/operator of S -75 shall maintain all records in an Air District -approved 
log. The owner/operator shall retain the records with the equipment for two years 
from the date of entry and make them available for inspection by Air District staff 
upon req uest. These recordkeeping requirements shall not replace the 
recordkeeping requirements in any applicable Air District Regulations.  
[Basis: Cumulative Increase, Regulation 2 -5] 
 
6. Within 30 days after the end of each calendar year, the owner/operator of S -75 shall provide 
the Director of the Air District’s Compliance and Enforcement Division a year -end summary 
with the following information :  
a. The location(s) and dates at which the engine was operated.  
b. The total amount of diesel fuel consumed by the engine in the previous 12 months 
(in gallons) . 
c. The total hours of operation.  
[Basis: Regulation 2 -1-403] 
 
 
 9  
 
 
End of Conditions

blocks.heading: Recommendation
blocks.text:
Recommendation
on  
The Air District has evaluated the permit application for the proposed project and has made a 
preliminary determination that the project is expected to comply with all applicable Air District, 
state, and federal air quality -related regulations, including t he health risks resulting from toxic air 
contaminant emissions. The preliminary recommendation is to issue a permit for this project. After 
considering all comments received, the Air District will make a final determination.  
 
I recommend that the Air District initiate the public comment period and consider any comments 
received prior to taking any final action on issuance of an Authority to Construct for the following 
source:  
 
 
S-75 Portable Prime Water Pump Diesel Engine  
Tier 4 Final with integrated SCR; Make: John Deere ; Model: 6068CI550 ;  
Model Year: 2024 
277 bhp,  1.61 MMBtu/hr  
CARB Executive Order: U -R-004-0676  
 
 
 
Prepared By:   Kevin Perez , Air Quality Engineer  I 
Date:   July 2, 202 5

unmapped_blocks.heading: TBACT
unmapped_blocks.text:
TBACT
CT  
Although S -75 results in a source risk that exceeds the Regulation 2 -5-301 TBACT threshold of 
1.0 in a million cancer risk, TBACT is not required for S -75 because it  is a portable non -road 
engine . Per Section 209(e) of the Federal Clean Air Act, “political subdivisions” shall not impose 
emission controls on non -road engines. The exception is for standards and other requirements 
imposed by the State of California necessary to achieve attainment of air pollution standards.  
 
Regulation 6, Rule 1 – Particulate Matter - General Requirements  
6-1-303 – Ringelmann No. 2 Limitation and 6 -1-305 – Visible Particles  
S-75 is subject to Regulation 6, Rule 1. Opacity and visible emissions from S-75 are limited by 
Regulation 6 -1-303.1 (internal combustion engines of less than 25 liters displacement). S-75 has a 
displacement of 6.8 liters.  
 
Section 6 -1-303.1 prohibits S-75 from  emitting for more than 3 minutes per hour visible emissions 
as dark or darker than Ringelmann No. 2 or equivalent opacity.  
 
 
 6 Regulation 6 -1-305 prohibits emission of particles from any operation in sufficient number to 
cause annoyance to any other person where the particles are large enough to be visible as individual 
particles at the emission point, or of such size and nature as to be visible  individually as 
incandescent particles.  S -75 is not expected to produce visible emissions or fallout in violation of 
this regulation and will be assumed to be in compliance with Regulation 6 -1-305. 
 
S-75 will comply with 0.15  grains per dry standard cubic feet  (gr/dscf ) in Section 6 -1-310.1, as 
shown below:  
 
PM 10 emission rate = 0. 0092 lb/hr (Refer to Table 2);  
 
Exhaust flow rate  = 136.15  dscfm  (194.5  acfm  @ 200 °F and 12.5% moisture content );  
Outlet grain loading rate = 0. 008 gr/dscf ( 0.0092 x 7000 ÷ 136.15  x 60) 
 
Sections 6 -1-310.2 and 6 -1-311.2 don’t apply  to S-75, since the PTE of engine to emit total 
suspended particulate ( TSP) is significantly lower than 1,000 kilograms per hour ( kg/hr ). Also, the 
above sections apply to processes that handle or process materials (not ICE).  
 
Compliance with the above standards in Regulation 6, Rule 1 will be confirmed by Air District 
Compliance and Enforcement staff during routine inspections of the facility.  
 
Regulation 9, Rule 1 – Inorganic Gaseous Pollutants - Sulfur Dioxide  
9-1-301 – Limitation on Ground Level Concentration  
S-75 is subject to and expected to comply with the applicable SO 2 limitations in Regulation 9, Rule 
1 (“Inorganic Gaseous Pollutants - Sulfur Dioxide”). Because SO 2 emissions from S-75 are 
negligible ( 5 lb/year), “The Pittsburg Owner LPV, LLC ” will not be required to conduct ground 
level monitoring of SO 2. 
 
9-1-304 – Fuel Burning (Liquid and Solid Fuels)  
Section 9 -1-304 states “A person shall not burn any liquid fuel having a sulfur content in excess 
of 0.5% by weight,” Section 209 (e) of the Federal Clean Air Act does not allow states or “political 
subdivisions” to impose emission control on non -road engi nes except when standards are 
necessary to achieve attainment of an air pollution standard, but does allow for regulating the hours 
of operation, daily emissions, and sulfur limits in the fuel. The California Air Resources Board 
(CARB) requires all diesel engines to burn Ultra Low Sulfur Diesel with a sulfur content of 15 
ppm by weight or less (≤ 0.0015% by wt.), which is less than 0.5% by wt. requirement in Section 
9-1-304. The requirement to ensure S -75 exclusively burn CARB diesel (~ULSD) is memorialized 
in Part 2 of the proposed permit condition # 100705. Therefore, S -75 will comply with 9 -1-304. 
 
Regulation 9, Rule 8 – Nitrogen Oxides and Carbon Monoxides from Stationary Internal 
Combustion Engines  
Since S -75 is portable, the  engine  is subject to Section 209(e) of the Federal Clean Air Act and is 
not subject to Regulation 9 -8 which applies to stationary ICE.
